name: HLA-E/NKG2A blockade boosted senescent-cell immune response
summary: Human tissue and in-vitro evidence identify an immune-evasion checkpoint on senescent cells, but not yet an in-vivo aging therapy.
statement: Senescent dermal fibroblasts expressed HLA-E, HLA-E was increased on senescent cells in old human skin and melanocytic nevi, and blocking HLA-E/NKG2A signaling boosted NK and CD8 T-cell responses against senescent cells in vitro.
population_or_model: Human dermal fibroblast models, old human skin sections, and melanocytic nevi
time_horizon: Cross-sectional human tissue and in-vitro immune-response assays
quantitative_note: The source reports increased HLA-E expression in old human skin relative to young skin and in nevi relative to normal skin, plus improved immune responses after checkpoint blockade in vitro.
caveats: The blockade signal is in vitro and does not establish in-vivo senescent-cell clearance.
Checkpoint modulation may increase immune activity beyond senescent cells.
This supports human relevance of the mechanism, not a human intervention stage upgrade.
